decision: Use SEC bulk Form N-PORT Data Sets (quarterly ZIPs of tab-delimited files) as the primary data source.
rationale: Verified against SEC primary sources: pre-parsed, joinable flat files covering fund identity, monthly returns, and holdings. Avoids scraping individual XML filings entirely.

decision: Identify the US equity fund universe using Yahoo Finance categories as a pre-filter, cross-checked against holdings-based inference (value-weighted ASSET_CAT='EC' + INVESTMENT_COUNTRY='US').
rationale: N-PORT has no fund-level equity/strategy label, so it must be inferred from holdings. Yahoo categories (joined via SEC's official mutual-fund ticker map) give a fast, independent cross-check, but are treated as a heuristic since yfinance is an unofficial scraper.

decision: Build peer groups ourselves via cosine similarity + clustering over holdings-based strategy vectors, then validate the clusters against Yahoo fund categories (purity / adjusted Rand index).
rationale: Yahoo categories could have been used directly as peer groups, but that would skip building real holdings-based similarity/clustering. Using them as a validator instead keeps the clustering work while still getting an external sanity check.

decision: Define 'underperform' as a fund's next-quarter total return falling below its cluster's median return, evaluated across multiple pooled quarterly prediction periods (not a single train/test split).
rationale: N-PORT holdings snapshots are effectively quarterly (only the 3rd month of each quarter is historically public), but each filing reports all 3 monthly returns — this maps cleanly onto a quarter-over-quarter prediction setup with several periods pooled for a realistic time-based split.

decision: Use phi-4 (Q4_K_S) served locally by LM Studio for plain-English narration of results only (RAG-style, grounded in computed data) — not for training or fine-tuning.
rationale: The quantized GGUF model cannot be trained, and the core data (fund holdings/returns) is numeric — a poor fit for LLM prediction. The LLM's strength here is explaining results already produced by classical ML, not producing the predictions itself.

decision: Run the project as a step-wise-methodology build: a contract-first plan.md, one gated milestone at a time, each with its own design.md and a human approval gate before the next step starts.
rationale: Keeps each stage reviewable and reversible, and avoids docs drifting from code since decisions/workflow docs update in the same change as each step.

decision: Set the fund universe target to 1000 resolved funds, using an iterative-fill sampling strategy (shuffle the full complete-panel candidate pool, resolve ticker+Yahoo data one at a time until 1000 succeed) rather than a fixed pre-filter sample.
rationale: A 20-fund pilot run showed ~45% of candidate series have no ticker mapping at all before Yahoo is even attempted; a fixed sample of exactly max_funds candidates would under-shoot the target after attrition. The complete 12-quarter panel has 9,445 candidate series, giving ample headroom.

decision: Corrected the US-equity flag to require BOTH yahoo_stock_position >= 0.80 (asset class) AND a holdings-based US-domiciled-equity share >= 0.70 (geography), instead of the asset-class signal alone.
rationale: Empirically confirmed yfinance's stockPosition only measures stock-vs-bond allocation, not geography - international and emerging-market equity funds also show stockPosition near 1.0. Combining it with a value-weighted holdings check (ASSET_CAT='EC' + INVESTMENT_COUNTRY='US') fixed 87/90 misflagged international funds in testing; the 3 remaining exceptions trace to a documented, accepted N-PORT data limitation (fund-of-funds wrappers reporting their issuer's country, not their economic exposure).

decision: Rejected category-only feature vectors (asset_cat/issuer_type/country) for fund clustering, in favor of building strategy vectors from actual portfolio overlap - since the step2 universe is already equity-filtered, category fields alone are nearly identical across all 363 funds and carry almost no discriminative signal.
rationale: Caught before implementation: clustering on near-identical category vectors would produce a degenerate result (one dominant cluster), defeating the purpose of peer grouping.

decision: Built and empirically tested issuer-overlap vectors (value-weighted, then breadth-weighted top-K held issuers) for clustering, then rejected them in favor of local text-embeddings of a holdings description (sentence-transformers, all-MiniLM-L6-v2).
rationale: Issuer-overlap vectors scored near chance-level agreement with Yahoo fund categories (adjusted Rand Index 0.01-0.07, confirmed insensitive to K and to cluster count 5-60 - ruled out as a tuning problem). Embeddings of a text description of each fund's top holdings raised ARI to ~0.25, roughly a 10x improvement, while running fully locally and fast (~60s for the full 12-quarter pipeline). Root cause of the overlap approach's failure: popular mega-cap stocks are held broadly across growth/value/blend categories, so raw stock overlap mostly captures 'which companies are popular' rather than value/growth style, which text embeddings apparently capture better.

decision: Added a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/tests.yml) running the fast pytest suite on every push/PR to main. Confirmed no CI/CD existed previously (no .github/workflows directory, and 3 commits sat unpushed on origin before this).
rationale: The 10 unit/smoke tests are fast (~5s) and need no network access or secrets (verified by running them with the data/ directory temporarily removed, simulating a clean checkout), making them well-suited to CI. The full pipeline (step1 ingestion: ~15+ min, GBs of downloads, needs SEC_USER_AGENT and live external APIs) deliberately stays out of CI and is only run manually per gated step, per the step-wise-methodology approach used throughout this project.
